does: This paper demonstrates a vulnerability in prompt-injection defenses where attackers distribute weak malicious directives across multiple events in a conversation, keeping each individual message below detection thresholds. The authors propose a temporal accumulation approach that aggregates per-event detector scores using peak and CUSUM persistence statistics to identify these 'slow-burn' attacks. They evaluate their method using a held-out autoregressive cloaking target to generate adversarial examples, then test against a frozen detector consisting of a char-ngram SVM and embedding-contrastive head. Results show that trajectory-level accumulation statistically outperforms per-event detection on concentrated attacks with a gap of 0.092 (95% CI [+0.025, +0.155]), and successfully identifies persistent attack patterns in repository exfiltration scenarios that differ from benign conversation patterns.
connects: This work builds on foundational research in adversarial prompt injection attacks against language models and tool-using agents, extending beyond single-event detection paradigms. It responds to the growing body of work on evasion attacks that operate under detection constraints, where adversaries strategically modify inputs to stay beneath classifier thresholds. The paper connects to both adversarial NLP literature and multi-turn dialogue security research, combining temporal signal processing techniques (CUSUM, peak detection) with machine learning security concepts. It also engages with proxy-side defense mechanisms and the broader challenge of detecting distributed attacks where individual components appear benign but exhibit coordinated malicious intent across sequences.
enables: This research opens directions for designing temporally-aware defense systems against sophisticated multi-turn attacks on agentic systems. It points toward deployable detectors that model attack patterns as temporal processes rather than isolated events, relevant as AI assistants with tool-use capabilities become more prevalent. The work suggests incorporating persistence detection and accumulation statistics into real-time monitoring systems for safety-critical applications. Future work could extend these methods to adaptive attackers who account for temporal defenses, develop online learning variants that don't require frozen models, and integrate trajectory-level analysis into agent frameworks. This also motivates research into fundamental tradeoffs between per-event and temporal detection and into attack strategies that deliberately optimize for slow-burn penetration of accumulated-score defenses.
